texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Over the last decade, increasing public awareness and concern about environmental and health issues have given rise to various technological solutions in environmental engineering. As part of this change, considerable progress in membrane technology for gas separation has been made  [1,2]. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The major gaseous pollutants emitted during the combustion of fossil fuels, during energy-intensive production of  materials, and during waste processing are CO2, SO2,  and NOx. The combined efforts of industry and the research  community  aim  to  intensify  their  capture  and  decrease  their  emissions,  with  the  final  objective  of meeting stricter emission limits, which is becoming more challenging with conventional technologies. Membrane separation  technology  is  a  particularly  significant  environmental  protection  application  because  it  can  be effectively combined with other separation technologies and generally uses much less energy. As membrane processes represent an energy-efficient alternative to traditional separation processes, in view of today ' s emissions policy, they have become economically competitive to purify flue gases emitted from small point sources, such as waste incineration units and steel or cement production facilities.
texts.text: The major gaseous pollutants emitted during the combustion of fossil fuels, during energy-intensive production of  materials, and during waste processing are CO2, SO2,  and NOx. The combined efforts of industry and the research  community  aim  to  intensify  their  capture  and  decrease  their  emissions,  with  the  final  objective  of meeting stricter emission limits, which is becoming more challenging with conventional technologies. Membrane separation  technology  is  a  particularly  significant  environmental  protection  application  because  it  can  be effectively combined with other separation technologies and generally uses much less energy. As membrane processes represent an energy-efficient alternative to traditional separation processes, in view of today ' s emissions policy, they have become economically competitive to purify flue gases emitted from small point sources, such as waste incineration units and steel or cement production facilities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Different membrane types with various specific functions, such as high solubility for certain gases, thermal and mechanical stability, and ease of manufacturing, are presented as perspective technologies for flue gas purification. Unique systems are also discussed, including facilitated or retarded transport of permeants in membranes and mixed-matrix membranes. We first describe the basic operating mechanism based on their structure and material properties to compare the advantages and disadvantages of suitable membranes. This review presents state-of-the-art membrane technologies in CO2, SO2, and NOx  separation from flue gases and discusses the potential applications of the most promising membranes.

This review presents state-of-the-art membrane technologies in CO2, SO2, and NOx  separation from flue gases and discusses the potential applications of the most promising membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Regarding the consideration of CO2 as a pollutant, the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) and European Union (EU) agencies nowadays use this classification (in Fig. 1). However, no limits on the amount of CO2 have been introduced from emitting installations; regulation mechanisms are in action. Scientifically, CO2  is associated with global warming and climate change; SO2 with acid rain and winter smog; and NOx  with acid rain, the formation of photochemical smog, and ozone depletion. Their increasing emission levels are the fundamental problem of atmospheric pollution due to increasing evidence of their adverse effect on public health.
texts.text: Regarding the consideration of CO2 as a pollutant, the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) and European Union (EU) agencies nowadays use this classification (in Fig. 1). However, no limits on the amount of CO2 have been introduced from emitting installations; regulation mechanisms are in action. Scientifically, CO2  is associated with global warming and climate change; SO2 with acid rain and winter smog; and NOx  with acid rain, the formation of photochemical smog, and ozone depletion. Their increasing emission levels are the fundamental problem of atmospheric pollution due to increasing evidence of their adverse effect on public health.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Conventional technologies for removing these three gases from flue gas are based on different approaches. For instance, the typical way to capture SO2 emissions proceeds via wet scrubbing in which SO2 is converted to products such as CaSO4; NOx removal processes are based on the catalytic reduction or the control of combustion conditions to lower its content in situ. Compared to these widespread technologies, amine scrubbing is the most common process for CO2  capture. Nevertheless, CO2 mitigation is very scarcely used [8]. The problems and challenges associated with CO2, SO2, and NOx capture differ considerably. Removal of SO2  and NOx  means a further reduction of the already low concentrations of these gases in the flue gas. In the case of CO2, vast amounts of the gas must be captured to achieve sufficiently low concentrations of CO2  in  the  cleaned  flue  gas  that  is  emitted  into  the  atmosphere.  In addition,  reaching  a  sufficiently  high  purity  of  the  captured  gas  is necessary to enable its further reuse or compression and transport for underground storage. The latter is why the purity of the final stream of separated CO2 is of great importance [9]. In all three cases, the gradual tightening  of  emission  targets  (as  shown  in  the  next  section)  makes attaining the environmental limits more difficult.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  successful  application  of  membrane  technology  to  simultaneously remove all three pollutants would make the flue gas treatment process less energy -demanding. Moreover, using modular membrane gas absorbers is a suitable economic strategy on a small scale where wet scrubbers  have  more  significant  investment  costs.  While  membranes have been successfully applied for CO2 separation in biogas upgrading, even in this field, removing trace impurities relies on classical adsorption or absorption processes [10]. Similar needs for their simultaneous removal exist  in  flue  gas  purification,  and  an  overview  of  the  latest advancements is given in the focus of this review article.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1. Emission of SO2
texts.text: 1.1. Emission of SO2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In 2010, the EU added new standards for SO2  emissions in the Industrial  Emission Directive (IED). For large combustion plants above 300 MW, emission limit values (ELVs) are as stringent as 35 mg/Nm 3 for gaseous fuels and 150 mg/Nm 3 for liquid and solid fuels (hence 25 % lower than the previous ELVs) [11]. In 2018, the largest emitter of SO2 among European countries was Poland (0.5 Mton), followed by Germany (0.3 Mton) and Spain (0.2 Mton). In 2016 the total emission of SO2 in Europe reached 2 Mton. Between 2016 and 2017, a reduction in SO2 emissions  was  reported,  and  the  total  reduction  in  EU  emissions amounted to only 1.2 %. Compared with the emission of 2005, where the level was 7 Mton, an overall reduction of 69 % was achieved [12]. The  energy  production  sector  was  identified  as  the  most  significant source of SO2 anthropogenic emissions [13].
texts.text: In 2010, the EU added new standards for SO2  emissions in the Industrial  Emission Directive (IED). For large combustion plants above 300 MW, emission limit values (ELVs) are as stringent as 35 mg/Nm 3 for gaseous fuels and 150 mg/Nm 3 for liquid and solid fuels (hence 25 % lower than the previous ELVs) [11]. In 2018, the largest emitter of SO2 among European countries was Poland (0.5 Mton), followed by Germany (0.3 Mton) and Spain (0.2 Mton). In 2016 the total emission of SO2 in Europe reached 2 Mton. Between 2016 and 2017, a reduction in SO2 emissions  was  reported,  and  the  total  reduction  in  EU  emissions amounted to only 1.2 %. Compared with the emission of 2005, where the level was 7 Mton, an overall reduction of 69 % was achieved [12]. The  energy  production  sector  was  identified  as  the  most  significant source of SO2 anthropogenic emissions [13].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to  the  US  EPA,  the  2020  annual  data  showed  a  19  % decline in SO2  emissions compared to 2019, also due to the economic slowdown from the COVID-19 pandemic. From 1995 to 2020, annual emissions of SO2 from power plants decreased from 12 Mton to 0.7 Mton [14].
texts.text: According to  the  US  EPA,  the  2020  annual  data  showed  a  19  % decline in SO2  emissions compared to 2019, also due to the economic slowdown from the COVID-19 pandemic. From 1995 to 2020, annual emissions of SO2 from power plants decreased from 12 Mton to 0.7 Mton [14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The total emissions of SO2 from Asia in 2010 -2015 increased by 46 Mton. The massive increase in SO2 emissions was due to coal combustion. On the other hand, the emissions of SO2 and NOx in Japan reported in 2015 decreased so distinctly that they contributed only about 1.5 and 4 % to Asia ' s total emissions, respectively [15].

On the other hand, the emissions of SO2 and NOx in Japan reported in 2015 decreased so distinctly that they contributed only about 1.5 and 4 % to Asia ' s total emissions, respectively [15].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1.1. Conventional methods of flue gas desulfurization
texts.text: 1.1.1. Conventional methods of flue gas desulfurization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are several common approaches to the removal of SO2  from flue  gas.  One  commonly used method is wet flue gas desulfurization (WFGD). WFGD involves passing the flue gas through an absorber tower, where it  comes  into  contact  with  a  wet  slurry  containing  a  sorbent,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: typically limestone or lime. A typical product of this process is gypsum, and WFGD is a significant supplier for the construction industry in many countries. The reaction is facilitated by the presence of water and a high surface area provided by the slurry. WFGD systems efficiently remove SO2 from flue gas and achieve up to 90 % removal efficiency. However, their efficiency is typically set according to emission limits to optimize the cost of flue gas treatment. The advantages of WFGD are high efficiency and low operating cost, but the capital costs are relatively high compared to alternatives [16].
texts.text: typically limestone or lime. A typical product of this process is gypsum, and WFGD is a significant supplier for the construction industry in many countries. The reaction is facilitated by the presence of water and a high surface area provided by the slurry. WFGD systems efficiently remove SO2 from flue gas and achieve up to 90 % removal efficiency. However, their efficiency is typically set according to emission limits to optimize the cost of flue gas treatment. The advantages of WFGD are high efficiency and low operating cost, but the capital costs are relatively high compared to alternatives [16].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another method for SO2  removal is dry sorption, which does not involve using a liquid slurry. Dry sorption techniques typically utilize solid  sorbents,  such  as  calcium-based  and  sodium-based  sorbents,  to capture SO2 directly from flue gas. The sorbents are injected into the flue gas stream, and the SO2 molecules are adsorbed onto the surface of the sorbent particles. Dry sorption offers several advantages over wet flue gas desulfurization, including lower water consumption. However, dry sorption processes may have lower SO2 removal efficiencies and higher operating costs compared to WFGD. Also, the spent sorbent may require further  treatment  or  disposal.  Despite  these  challenges,  dry  sorption technologies  are  being  actively  developed  and  improved  to  enhance their  efficiency  and  cost-effectiveness  for  SO2  removal from flue  gas [17].
texts.text: Another method for SO2  removal is dry sorption, which does not involve using a liquid slurry. Dry sorption techniques typically utilize solid  sorbents,  such  as  calcium-based  and  sodium-based  sorbents,  to capture SO2 directly from flue gas. The sorbents are injected into the flue gas stream, and the SO2 molecules are adsorbed onto the surface of the sorbent particles. Dry sorption offers several advantages over wet flue gas desulfurization, including lower water consumption. However, dry sorption processes may have lower SO2 removal efficiencies and higher operating costs compared to WFGD. Also, the spent sorbent may require further  treatment  or  disposal.  Despite  these  challenges,  dry  sorption technologies  are  being  actively  developed  and  improved  to  enhance their  efficiency  and  cost-effectiveness  for  SO2  removal from flue  gas [17].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Semi-dry  and  semi-wet  desulfurization  are  hybrid  approaches combining wet and dry methods for removing SO2 from flue gas. In semidry desulfurization, a spray dryer injects a slurry or absorbent solution into  the  flue  gas  stream.  The  flue  gas  comes  into  contact  with  the droplets, allowing for the absorption of SO2. The partially saturated flue gas is then passed through a fabric filter to remove the absorbed SO2 and particulate matter. Semiwet desulfurization, on the other hand, involves the injection of a dry sorbent into the flue gas followed by adding a limited amount of liquid, typically water or reagent solution, to create a humid environment for the sorbent. This enables the chemical reaction between  the  sorbent  and  SO2  to  take  place.  Semi-dry  and  semi-wet desulfurization methods offer advantages such as reduced water consumption compared to wet methods and higher SO2 removal efficiencies compared to dry methods. These hybrid approaches provide flexibility regarding system design and can be tailored to suit specific plant requirements and emission regulations [18,19].
texts.text: Semi-dry  and  semi-wet  desulfurization  are  hybrid  approaches combining wet and dry methods for removing SO2 from flue gas. In semidry desulfurization, a spray dryer injects a slurry or absorbent solution into  the  flue  gas  stream.  The  flue  gas  comes  into  contact  with  the droplets, allowing for the absorption of SO2. The partially saturated flue gas is then passed through a fabric filter to remove the absorbed SO2 and particulate matter. Semiwet desulfurization, on the other hand, involves the injection of a dry sorbent into the flue gas followed by adding a limited amount of liquid, typically water or reagent solution, to create a humid environment for the sorbent. This enables the chemical reaction between  the  sorbent  and  SO2  to  take  place.  Semi-dry  and  semi-wet desulfurization methods offer advantages such as reduced water consumption compared to wet methods and higher SO2 removal efficiencies compared to dry methods. These hybrid approaches provide flexibility regarding system design and can be tailored to suit specific plant requirements and emission regulations [18,19].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The choice of the type of flue gas desulfurization system is typically associated with the size of the plant. More significant facilities typically favor wet solutions with higher capital costs and lower operating costs.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2. Emission of CO2
texts.text: 1.2. Emission of CO2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of  the  most  significant  contributors  to  the  emissions  of  CO2 globally is the energy sector, but the transfer of energy production towards  sustainable  solutions  is  a  prolonged  and  challenging  process. Therefore,  post-combustion  carbon  capture  and  storage  (CCS)  is  a crucial strategy to bridge this transfer period and allow the fulfilment of environmental  targets  today  regulated  by  various  systems  of  carbon taxes.
texts.text: One of  the  most  significant  contributors  to  the  emissions  of  CO2 globally is the energy sector, but the transfer of energy production towards  sustainable  solutions  is  a  prolonged  and  challenging  process. Therefore,  post-combustion  carbon  capture  and  storage  (CCS)  is  a crucial strategy to bridge this transfer period and allow the fulfilment of environmental  targets  today  regulated  by  various  systems  of  carbon taxes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The trend of CO2 emissions differs significantly in different regions. According to the US EPA, from 1995 to 2020, CO2 emissions declined by 27 %. Nevertheless, emissions remain high at 1.57 Gton (2020) [14]. Almost the same results were achieved in Europe, where CO2 emissions decreased by more than 26 % between 1991 and 2014, from 2.02 Gton to 1.5 Gton [20]. In contrast, all air pollutant emissions in Asia increased significantly  during  the  last  six  decades.  The  cumulative  2015 -2019 emissions of CO2  in Asia represented 18.3 Gton. Growth rates of CO2 emissions were relatively large because of the power plants, industries, and transportation, for which fuel consumption increased significantly, along with economic development in Asia. This increase in CO2  emissions was especially significant in the early 2000 s, with the considerable contribution of China [15].
texts.text: The trend of CO2 emissions differs significantly in different regions. According to the US EPA, from 1995 to 2020, CO2 emissions declined by 27 %. Nevertheless, emissions remain high at 1.57 Gton (2020) [14]. Almost the same results were achieved in Europe, where CO2 emissions decreased by more than 26 % between 1991 and 2014, from 2.02 Gton to 1.5 Gton [20]. In contrast, all air pollutant emissions in Asia increased significantly  during  the  last  six  decades.  The  cumulative  2015 -2019 emissions of CO2  in Asia represented 18.3 Gton. Growth rates of CO2 emissions were relatively large because of the power plants, industries, and transportation, for which fuel consumption increased significantly, along with economic development in Asia. This increase in CO2  emissions was especially significant in the early 2000 s, with the considerable contribution of China [15].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2.1. Conventional methods of post-combustion carbon capture
texts.text: 1.2.1. Conventional methods of post-combustion carbon capture

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  topic  of  post-combustion  carbon  capture  is  relatively  new. Therefore, there are no approaches that can be considered conventional. Several methods can be considered for this purpose. The most tested and commonly used method involves using a solvent, such as amine solutions, to absorb CO2 from flue gas. The solvent reacts with CO2, forming a chemical compound that stays in the solvent. The solvent is later regenerated and reused. The most common form of this method is amine absorption.
texts.text: The  topic  of  post-combustion  carbon  capture  is  relatively  new. Therefore, there are no approaches that can be considered conventional. Several methods can be considered for this purpose. The most tested and commonly used method involves using a solvent, such as amine solutions, to absorb CO2 from flue gas. The solvent reacts with CO2, forming a chemical compound that stays in the solvent. The solvent is later regenerated and reused. The most common form of this method is amine absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another approach is based on adsorption. A good example is calcium looping. Calcium looping involves using calcium oxide (CaO) as a sorbent to capture CO2. The process begins by ex-posing flue gas containing CO2 to the sorbent. The calcium oxide reacts with CO2, forming calcium carbonate (CaCO3). The calcium carbonate is then separated from the flue gas and undergoes a regeneration step where it is heated, CO2  is released, and regenerated calcium oxide can be used again.
texts.text: Another approach is based on adsorption. A good example is calcium looping. Calcium looping involves using calcium oxide (CaO) as a sorbent to capture CO2. The process begins by ex-posing flue gas containing CO2 to the sorbent. The calcium oxide reacts with CO2, forming calcium carbonate (CaCO3). The calcium carbonate is then separated from the flue gas and undergoes a regeneration step where it is heated, CO2  is released, and regenerated calcium oxide can be used again.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally,  there  is  membrane  separation,  which  is  the  topic  of  this paper.
texts.text: Finally,  there  is  membrane  separation,  which  is  the  topic  of  this paper.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2.2. Conventional methods of controlling the emissions of NOx
texts.text: 1.2.2. Conventional methods of controlling the emissions of NOx

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Various methods can be used to avoid the emissions of NOx. These methods can be divided into two categories, typically used in combination: primary and secondary.
texts.text: Various methods can be used to avoid the emissions of NOx. These methods can be divided into two categories, typically used in combination: primary and secondary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Primary measures of NOx  emission control focus on reducing the formation of nitrogen oxides during the combustion process. One of the key approaches is optimizing combustion parameters, such as controlling the air-to-fuel ratio. Maintaining a stoichiometric or slightly fuelrich  condition  limits  the  amount  of  available  oxygen,  resulting  in lower peak temperatures and reduced NOx formation. Another primary measure is using  low-NOx  burners,  designed  to  create  more  uniform mixing of fuel and air, thus reducing the formation of high-temperature pockets  where  NOx is  formed.  Additionally,  advanced  combustion technologies, such as staged combustion and lean-burn combustion, are employed  to  minimize  the  peak  flame  temperature  and  inhibit  the generation of NOx. These primary measures aim to address NOx  emissions at the source and are crucial for significantly reducing NOx levels in various industrial processes and power generation facilities.
texts.text: Primary measures of NOx  emission control focus on reducing the formation of nitrogen oxides during the combustion process. One of the key approaches is optimizing combustion parameters, such as controlling the air-to-fuel ratio. Maintaining a stoichiometric or slightly fuelrich  condition  limits  the  amount  of  available  oxygen,  resulting  in lower peak temperatures and reduced NOx formation. Another primary measure is using  low-NOx  burners,  designed  to  create  more  uniform mixing of fuel and air, thus reducing the formation of high-temperature pockets  where  NOx is  formed.  Additionally,  advanced  combustion technologies, such as staged combustion and lean-burn combustion, are employed  to  minimize  the  peak  flame  temperature  and  inhibit  the generation of NOx. These primary measures aim to address NOx  emissions at the source and are crucial for significantly reducing NOx levels in various industrial processes and power generation facilities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Secondary measures of NOx  emission control focus on treating the flue gas. After it has been generated, the goal is to remove or reduce the concentration of nitrogen oxides. One widely used secondary measure is selective  catalytic  reduction  (SCR),  which  involves  the  injection  of ammonia  or  urea-based  compounds  into  the  flue  gas  stream.  In  the presence of a catalyst, the NOx reacts with the ammonia to form nitrogen and water vapor. Another secondary measure is selective non-catalytic reduction (SNCR), which involves injecting ammonia or urea directly into the flue gas without a catalyst. This technique relies on high temperatures in the flue gas in the combustion chamber to promote the reduction of NOx.
texts.text: Secondary measures of NOx  emission control focus on treating the flue gas. After it has been generated, the goal is to remove or reduce the concentration of nitrogen oxides. One widely used secondary measure is selective  catalytic  reduction  (SCR),  which  involves  the  injection  of ammonia  or  urea-based  compounds  into  the  flue  gas  stream.  In  the presence of a catalyst, the NOx reacts with the ammonia to form nitrogen and water vapor. Another secondary measure is selective non-catalytic reduction (SNCR), which involves injecting ammonia or urea directly into the flue gas without a catalyst. This technique relies on high temperatures in the flue gas in the combustion chamber to promote the reduction of NOx.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.3. Emission of NOx
texts.text: 1.3. Emission of NOx

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the forms of nitrogen monoxide (NO) and nitrogen dioxide (NO2), NOx is emitted during fuel combustion, e.g., in industrial facilities and the  transport  sector.  While  NO  represents  over  95  %  of  total  NOx emissions,  NO2  is  mainly  formed via photochemical  reactions  in  the atmosphere [21]. It is estimated that between 12 % and 44 % of the nitrogen loading of coastal water bodies comes from the air.
texts.text: In the forms of nitrogen monoxide (NO) and nitrogen dioxide (NO2), NOx is emitted during fuel combustion, e.g., in industrial facilities and the  transport  sector.  While  NO  represents  over  95  %  of  total  NOx emissions,  NO2  is  mainly  formed via photochemical  reactions  in  the atmosphere [21]. It is estimated that between 12 % and 44 % of the nitrogen loading of coastal water bodies comes from the air.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: NOx  emissions in the EU were 5.5 Mton in 2020. Since 2016, all European countries have complied with their NOx emission ceilings. In absolute amounts, the largest emitters of NOx  in Europe in 2017 were Germany (1.3 Mton), followed by the United Kingdom (0.92 Mton) and France (0.8 Mton). In 2016, the annual NOx  emission of EU countries was at the level of 7 Mton/year. The total reduction in aggregated EU emissions  was  only  2.2  %  between  2016  and  2017,  with  an  overall decrease  of  38  %  since  2005  when  the  level  was  12  Mton  [12].  In contrast, additional efforts are needed for all flue gas pollutants if the EU

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Various methods can be used to avoid the emissions of NOx. These methods can be divided into two categories that are typically used in combination: primary measures and secondary measures.
texts.text: Various methods can be used to avoid the emissions of NOx. These methods can be divided into two categories that are typically used in combination: primary measures and secondary measures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Primary measures of NOx  emission control focus on reducing the formation of nitrogen oxides during the combustion process itself. One of the key approaches is through the optimization of combustion parameters,  such  as  controlling  the  air-to-fuel  ratio.  By  maintaining  a stoichiometric or slightly fuel-rich condition, the amount of available oxygen is limited, resulting in lower peak temperatures and reduced NOx formation.  Another  primary  measure  is  the  use  of  low-NOx  burners, which are designed to create more uniform mixing of fuel and air, thus reducing  the  formation  of  high-temperature  pockets  where  NOx is formed.  Additionally,  advanced  combustion  technologies,  such  as staged combustion and lean-burn combustion, are employed to minimize the peak flame temperature and inhibit the generation of NOx. These primary measures aim to address NOx emissions at the source and are crucial for achieving significant reductions in NOx levels in various industrial processes and power generation facilities [23].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  the  following  sections,  this  review  will  describe  the  chemical/ physical  and  surface  properties  of  the  most  suitable  membranes  and their performance for flue gas purification by removing SO2, NOx, and CO2. Addressing the advantages and disadvantages of membranes, we describe the basic operating principles related to their structure. This review explores the progress of membrane separation technologies in high-efficiency flue gas purification.

This review explores the progress of membrane separation technologies in high-efficiency flue gas purification.

texts.text: for  these  applications  [26 -30].  Development  and  optimization  of membranes for specific applications can identify the mechanism of flue gas  separation  and  the  conditions  under  which  they  become  most effective. Table 1 describes the common membrane types for gas purification and lists their main advantages and disadvantages.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Commercial  membrane  separation  processes  for  gas  and  vapor removal mostly operate at high pressures and high gas concentrations. The prohibitive cost for compression of large gas volumes means that only a low gas partial pressure difference can be used in flue gas separation, which provides a low driving force for gas separation. The low partial pressure of gases like CO2, especially SO2 and NOx, causes higher energy penalties, which is one of the limitations of capturing these gases from  flue  gas  streams  using  membranes  compared  to  conventional chemical absorption processes.

The prohibitive cost for compression of large gas volumes means that only a low gas partial pressure difference can be used in flue gas separation, which provides a low driving force for gas separation. The low partial pressure of gases like CO2, especially SO2 and NOx, causes higher energy penalties, which is one of the limitations of capturing these gases from  flue  gas  streams  using  membranes  compared  to  conventional chemical absorption processes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several different membrane module types are used for flue gas separation  in  experimental  work.  Researchers  commonly  use  plate-andframe (flat sheet), tubular, and hollow fibre membrane modules. Spiral wound modules are used scarcely in the laboratory, but industrially they are one of the two most used types (e.g. UOP Cyanara ® ). Table 2 summarizes the advantages and disadvantages of the various membrane module types.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most of the mentioned advantages and disadvantages apply to water purification or other liquid phase processes. The hollow fibre configuration is the most efficient for gas separations requiring high pressure. In contrast, the plate-and-frame configuration has the advantage of a lowpressure drop, which can be more critical for high-volume, low-pressure applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Problems with wetting in membrane contactors can adversely affect the mass-transfer coefficients in a module and increase the membrane resistance,  hence  deteriorating  the  absorption  performance. Different causes of membrane wetting can be discerned as those relevant to the process conditions and the liquid phase and related to the properties and parameters of the used membrane. The pressure and flow rate of the liquid represent two crucial parameters affecting the degree of membrane wetting, whereas higher pressure and flow rates lead to its increase [34]. Even a full wetting operation mode can be improved by decreasing  the  liquid  side ' s  operating  pressure.  Surface  tension  and viscosity  of  the  absorbent  also  affect  the  wetting  potential.  Namely, solutions with higher surface tension and viscosity are responsible for decreased membrane wetting. However, the absorbent properties are

texts.orig: strictly  defined  by  the  gas  separation  application  of  the  membrane contactor and the absorbed gas of interest. The concentration of the absorbent directly affects absorbing solution properties. For example, a higher content of organic compounds can decrease the solution surface tension and thus promote membrane wetting. In the case of ionic liquids (ILs), light gases such as CO2 can drastically affect the surface tension of the liquid and, thus, the contact angle [35]. Membrane properties have an  equal  determining  role  in  the  extent  of  the  wetting.  Namely,  the membrane surface energy is closely related to its hydrophobicity, and the membrane porosity and pore size distribution define the degree of wetting for any given absorbing liquid. Table 4 overviews the available ways of preventing membrane wetting in membrane contactors [36]. These involve selecting the materials or their post-modifications and using  the  most  appropriate  membrane  morphology  or  operating conditions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Mechanisms of flue gas membrane purification
texts.text: 2.3. Mechanisms of flue gas membrane purification

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main transport mechanisms that can occur in membranes suitable for one-phase gas separation are surface diffusion and molecular sieving (Fig. 3), the solution-diffusion mechanism (Fig. 4), or facilitated transport (Fig. 5). In all cases, the flue gas mixture, fed to the membrane device, passes along one side of the membrane (feed side). The opposite side of the membrane (permeate side) is held at a lower pressure or in sweeping gas mode. The pressure difference and concentration gradient across the membrane provide the driving force for the permeation of gas molecules through the membrane.
texts.text: The main transport mechanisms that can occur in membranes suitable for one-phase gas separation are surface diffusion and molecular sieving (Fig. 3), the solution-diffusion mechanism (Fig. 4), or facilitated transport (Fig. 5). In all cases, the flue gas mixture, fed to the membrane device, passes along one side of the membrane (feed side). The opposite side of the membrane (permeate side) is held at a lower pressure or in sweeping gas mode. The pressure difference and concentration gradient across the membrane provide the driving force for the permeation of gas molecules through the membrane.

texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 3, in the case of molecular sieving, the molecules with a smaller diameter than the average pore size of the rigid membrane material permeate across the membrane. In contrast, the larger molecules are excluded [38]. The selectivity is determined by the ratio between  the  molecular  and  pore  sizes  and  by  imperfections  in  the membrane. Simultaneously, suppose both gas molecules fit inside the pores.  In  that  case,  the  mechanism  can  be  different  and  entirely dependent on Knudsen ' s  selectivity,  where  gas molecules collide less often with other gas molecules and more often with the walls of the pores. texts.label: text
texts.orig: solution-diffusion  mechanism  (Fig.  4),  where  specific  gas  molecules dissolve  preferentially  in  the  bulk  of  the  non-porous  membrane  and subsequently diffuse to the downstream side. As a result, flue gas molecules must dissolve into the membrane on the feed side, diffuse through the dense film, and then desorb from the permeate side of the membrane. More polar gases interact with the membrane polymer matrix much stronger than non-polar gases [42], while heavier gases condense more  readily.  Thus,  the  solution-diffusion  mechanism  through  membranes generates selective permeation via preferential sorption, preferential diffusion, or both. Some polymeric materials, such as the polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs), are so stiff that they become highly size-selective  and  almost  behave  as  molecular  sieves  [43,44].  At  the same time, their high fractional free volume (FFV) and narrow pore size also favours selective sorption of gases such as CO2.
texts.text: solution-diffusion  mechanism  (Fig.  4),  where  specific  gas  molecules dissolve  preferentially  in  the  bulk  of  the  non-porous  membrane  and subsequently diffuse to the downstream side. As a result, flue gas molecules must dissolve into the membrane on the feed side, diffuse through the dense film, and then desorb from the permeate side of the membrane. More polar gases interact with the membrane polymer matrix much stronger than non-polar gases [42], while heavier gases condense more  readily.  Thus,  the  solution-diffusion  mechanism  through  membranes generates selective permeation via preferential sorption, preferential diffusion, or both. Some polymeric materials, such as the polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs), are so stiff that they become highly size-selective  and  almost  behave  as  molecular  sieves  [43,44].  At  the same time, their high fractional free volume (FFV) and narrow pore size also favours selective sorption of gases such as CO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Facilitated transport can occur through (a) mobile carrier migration in the direction of lower concentration of the gas or (b) jumping over several  fixed  carrier  sites.  According  to  these  two  mechanisms,  the presence of a carrier increases the solubility of the chemical species in the membrane or promotes the transfer speed by increasing the diffusion coefficients through membranes [45]. The carriers have an essential role in facilitated transport mechanisms [46]. Based on the type of carrier, facilitated transport membranes can be classified into two categories: fixed carrier membranes and mobile carrier membranes [47].
texts.text: Facilitated transport can occur through (a) mobile carrier migration in the direction of lower concentration of the gas or (b) jumping over several  fixed  carrier  sites.  According  to  these  two  mechanisms,  the presence of a carrier increases the solubility of the chemical species in the membrane or promotes the transfer speed by increasing the diffusion coefficients through membranes [45]. The carriers have an essential role in facilitated transport mechanisms [46]. Based on the type of carrier, facilitated transport membranes can be classified into two categories: fixed carrier membranes and mobile carrier membranes [47].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mobile carrier can move freely across the membrane as it first reacts with CO2 on the feed side, and the reaction product moves across the  membrane.  The  fixed  carriers  are  covalently  bonded  to  the membrane, and their mobility is limited. The CO2 molecule reacts with one carrier site and then hops to the following site until it reaches the permeate side [48]. Fig. 6 illustrates the two types of CO2  transport mechanisms in facilitated transport membranes: in addition to solutiondiffusion transport, CO2  reacts with active sites in the matrix, thereby chemically enhancing the CO2 flux across the membrane.
texts.text: The mobile carrier can move freely across the membrane as it first reacts with CO2 on the feed side, and the reaction product moves across the  membrane.  The  fixed  carriers  are  covalently  bonded  to  the membrane, and their mobility is limited. The CO2 molecule reacts with one carrier site and then hops to the following site until it reaches the permeate side [48]. Fig. 6 illustrates the two types of CO2  transport mechanisms in facilitated transport membranes: in addition to solutiondiffusion transport, CO2  reacts with active sites in the matrix, thereby chemically enhancing the CO2 flux across the membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The carriers could migrate in the membrane matrix in semi-mobile carrier  membranes,  but  the  polymer  chains  restrict  their  mobility. These membranes, like fixed carrier membranes, are solid-state membranes. In this case, the transport mechanisms are both the hopping and the vehicle mechanisms. Thus, semi-mobile carrier membranes combine the advantages of both fixed and mobile carrier membranes [47].
texts.text: The carriers could migrate in the membrane matrix in semi-mobile carrier  membranes,  but  the  polymer  chains  restrict  their  mobility. These membranes, like fixed carrier membranes, are solid-state membranes. In this case, the transport mechanisms are both the hopping and the vehicle mechanisms. Thus, semi-mobile carrier membranes combine the advantages of both fixed and mobile carrier membranes [47].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ionic liquids (ILs) offer an effective medium for forming facilitated transport membranes [49 -51]. In the context of CO2 complexation, Cu + , Fe 2 + , Pd 0 , Zn 2 + , and Co 2 + based complexes have been used as carriers in gas -liquid and gas -solid processes. Gas permeability increases with the carrier content in the membrane, and gas permeation occurs via molecular diffusion and facilitated transport [52]. Facilitated transport of CO2  and salting-out effects on N2  and CH4  result in high CO2  permeability and ideal selectivity. The CO2 permeability often decreases due to carrier saturation at high CO2  partial pressures [53]. In this case, the reaction rate is higher than the diffusion rate [54]. This helps to explain the high permeability of supported ionic liquid membranes (SILMs) for facilitated transport [55]. A new ' task-specific ' IL type can be used as carriers  in  facilitated  transport  membranes.  Task-specific  ILs  were designed to selectively react with target molecules, ensuring high and selective permeability of the target gas [56]. The concentration of the ILbased carriers is high, and the membrane can absorb large amounts of the  target  gas.  Furthermore,  no  solvent  is  needed  in  such  facilitated transport membranes [57].
texts.text: Ionic liquids (ILs) offer an effective medium for forming facilitated transport membranes [49 -51]. In the context of CO2 complexation, Cu + , Fe 2 + , Pd 0 , Zn 2 + , and Co 2 + based complexes have been used as carriers in gas -liquid and gas -solid processes. Gas permeability increases with the carrier content in the membrane, and gas permeation occurs via molecular diffusion and facilitated transport [52]. Facilitated transport of CO2  and salting-out effects on N2  and CH4  result in high CO2  permeability and ideal selectivity. The CO2 permeability often decreases due to carrier saturation at high CO2  partial pressures [53]. In this case, the reaction rate is higher than the diffusion rate [54]. This helps to explain the high permeability of supported ionic liquid membranes (SILMs) for facilitated transport [55]. A new ' task-specific ' IL type can be used as carriers  in  facilitated  transport  membranes.  Task-specific  ILs  were designed to selectively react with target molecules, ensuring high and selective permeability of the target gas [56]. The concentration of the ILbased carriers is high, and the membrane can absorb large amounts of the  target  gas.  Furthermore,  no  solvent  is  needed  in  such  facilitated transport membranes [57].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zhang et al. applied Zn 2 + and Ni 2 + ions as fixed carrier sites in a Pebax ® matrix. They reported that the existence of these carriers in the polymer matrix resulted in enhanced CO2 permeability (321 barrer) and CO2/N2  selectivity (42.8) [58]. Huang et al. [59] modified graphene oxide  (GO)  fillers  with  task-specific  ILs  and  prepared  NH2-IL-GO/ Pebax ® membranes. They reported that the amine groups in the IL serve as the fixed carrier sites and improved the CO2 permeability (50 %) and CO2/N2  selectivity  (90  %)  compared  to  the  pure  Pebax ® membrane [59]. Kasahara et al. prepared mobile carrier membranes using aminoacid-based  ILs  and  studied  the  effect  of  size  and  number  of  amino groups  on  membrane  performance.  They  concluded  that  the  membrane ' s FFV decreased with decreasing IL size, which can increase the CO2/N2 selectivity. They also stated that the CO2 absorption increased with higher concentrations of amino groups in the IL structure [60]. Investigation of the solubility and diffusion coefficients showed that the

texts.orig: Various  inorganic  membranes  have  been  studied  for  CO2  separations. One of the advantages of these materials is their high-temperature stability. Promising  CO2 permselective  inorganic  membranes  are ceramic/carbonate dual-phase membranes, porous zeolites, and inorganic silica membranes. They can be used in high-temperature separation  and  reaction  processes  for  CO2 capture  or  intensification.  As

texts.orig: reported by Chen et al. [65], the CO2  permeation flux decreases, and activation energy for permeation  increases  for  membranes  with increasing sulfate content in the molten phase in CO2  perm-selective ceramic/carbonate dual-phase membranes, which causes the decrease of CO2  permeation flux. Eiberger et al. [66] reported amino-modified mesoporous ceramic membranes ' CO2/N2  separation performance for separating flue gas from a coal power plant. The asymmetric membrane structure  was  achieved  by  using  macroporous α -Al2O3  support.  The ability to separate CO2/N2 gas mixtures was demonstrated under laboratory and real conditions. In the second case, a close-to-application test rig was installed in the flue gas duct after the desulfurization unit. The membranes faced temperatures between 60 and 70 ◦ C and moderate NOx,  SO2, and  dust  content  [66].  In  another  study,  for  selective adsorption of CO2, a sub-nanometer membrane was investigated based on a porous α -alumina support tube with an X-type zeolite. The membrane was applied to single gases (i.e., permeation with CO2, N2, CH4, C2H6, and SF6) and for separation of mixtures of CO2 and N2 or CO2 and CH4 [67].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1.2. Polymer membranes
texts.text: 3.1.2. Polymer membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymeric membranes have shown great potential in gas separation fields due to their easy preparation, cheap production on a larger scale, and wide range of possible applications [89]. Currently, from the scientific point of view, some of the most competitive membrane materials are PIMs. They have not yet found commercial applications because of practical limitations, such as their sometimes brittle nature and strong physical aging. The latter leads to long-term changes in their transport properties, with a gradually decreasing permeability and often increasing selectivity [74]. Despite the higher selectivity, the decreasing permeability is usually undesired, and there is a strong effort to find approaches to reduce physical aging [4] or, in some cases, to enhance it and use it as an advantage to achieve higher size selectivity and improve long-term stability [90]. Despite their limitations, the benefits of PIMs are their unique and intrinsic microporous structure [91], leading to high permeability, enhancing solubility, and diffusion selectivity [92]. The rigid and contorted molecular structure in PIMs is responsible for their unique microporous structure, preventing the ordered packing of the  polymer  chains  and  resulting  in  excellent  gas  permeability  but moderate  selectivity,  limiting  their  practical  application  [93].  The partially fluorinated ' PIM-2 ' ,  with  interconnected void structure and BET surface area greater than 600 m 2 g 1 ,  was used as a membrane material for CO2/N2 separation. The gas solubility in PIM-2 is relatively low compared to PIMs with similar high FFV since the F atoms prefer to establish  intra-  and  interchain  halogen  bonds rather  than interacting with the penetrant gas [70] (Table 5). Its fluorinated nature imparts a more hydrophobic nature and it is thus remarkably insensitive to water in the feed stream. Although PIMs are known to lose their high ideal selectivity  with  CO2/CH4 mixtures  at  elevated  pressures  [94],  the ultrapermeable PIM based on tetramethyl-tetrahydronaphthalene units coupled with bicyclic triptycene (PIM-TMN-Trip) shows excellent separation properties for model mixtures of CO2/CH4 and raw biogas. It was noticed that CO2  treatment at 5 bar strongly enhances the ideal selectivity  to  values  in  the  range  of  34 -39  without  reducing  the  CO2 permeability. Such behaviour typically occurs because CO2 has a higher solubility  than  methane,  and competitive  sorption takes place  in the system [95]. The departure of the CO2/CH4  permselectivity from the pure-gas  values  can  be  explained  by  a  decrease  in  the  size-sieving capability  of  the  membrane  [96].  The  presented  membranes  have good long-term performance under representative operation conditions [97] and considerable commercial potential. The same PIM-TMN-Trip was used for SO2  separation from flue gas and showed excellent gas separation properties under mixed gas conditions; the CO2/N2 mixed gas selectivity ranged from 11 to 18 [71]. The increase in the CO2 gas separation performance is a result of the high critical temperature of CO2, due to its quadrupole moment, compared with N2 [98], causing the CO2 to be more easily absorbed, favouring the selectivity for CO2.
texts.text: Polymeric membranes have shown great potential in gas separation fields due to their easy preparation, cheap production on a larger scale, and wide range of possible applications [89]. Currently, from the scientific point of view, some of the most competitive membrane materials are PIMs. They have not yet found commercial applications because of practical limitations, such as their sometimes brittle nature and strong physical aging. The latter leads to long-term changes in their transport properties, with a gradually decreasing permeability and often increasing selectivity [74]. Despite the higher selectivity, the decreasing permeability is usually undesired, and there is a strong effort to find approaches to reduce physical aging [4] or, in some cases, to enhance it and use it as an advantage to achieve higher size selectivity and improve long-term stability [90]. Despite their limitations, the benefits of PIMs are their unique and intrinsic microporous structure [91], leading to high permeability, enhancing solubility, and diffusion selectivity [92]. The rigid and contorted molecular structure in PIMs is responsible for their unique microporous structure, preventing the ordered packing of the  polymer  chains  and  resulting  in  excellent  gas  permeability  but moderate  selectivity,  limiting  their  practical  application  [93].  The partially fluorinated ' PIM-2 ' ,  with  interconnected void structure and BET surface area greater than 600 m 2 g 1 ,  was used as a membrane material for CO2/N2 separation. The gas solubility in PIM-2 is relatively low compared to PIMs with similar high FFV since the F atoms prefer to establish  intra-  and  interchain  halogen  bonds rather  than interacting with the penetrant gas [70] (Table 5). Its fluorinated nature imparts a more hydrophobic nature and it is thus remarkably insensitive to water in the feed stream. Although PIMs are known to lose their high ideal selectivity  with  CO2/CH4 mixtures  at  elevated  pressures  [94],  the ultrapermeable PIM based on tetramethyl-tetrahydronaphthalene units coupled with bicyclic triptycene (PIM-TMN-Trip) shows excellent separation properties for model mixtures of CO2/CH4 and raw biogas. It was noticed that CO2  treatment at 5 bar strongly enhances the ideal selectivity  to  values  in  the  range  of  34 -39  without  reducing  the  CO2 permeability. Such behaviour typically occurs because CO2 has a higher solubility  than  methane,  and competitive  sorption takes place  in the system [95]. The departure of the CO2/CH4  permselectivity from the pure-gas  values  can  be  explained  by  a  decrease  in  the  size-sieving capability  of  the  membrane  [96].  The  presented  membranes  have good long-term performance under representative operation conditions [97] and considerable commercial potential. The same PIM-TMN-Trip was used for SO2  separation from flue gas and showed excellent gas separation properties under mixed gas conditions; the CO2/N2 mixed gas selectivity ranged from 11 to 18 [71]. The increase in the CO2 gas separation performance is a result of the high critical temperature of CO2, due to its quadrupole moment, compared with N2 [98], causing the CO2 to be more easily absorbed, favouring the selectivity for CO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Adsorption of CO2 from gas mixtures near coal- and gas-fired power plants was reported by Budd et al. [99]. The CO2 and N2 permeabilities decreased to 25 % and 15 %, respectively. No plasticization effect of CO2 was observed under dry and wet conditions [69]. However, PIMs are known for their physical aging caused by the gradual collapse of the free volume elements, which could substantially decrease gas permeability in  time.  At  the  same  time,  their  sorption  properties  are  almost  unchanged [100].
texts.text: Adsorption of CO2 from gas mixtures near coal- and gas-fired power plants was reported by Budd et al. [99]. The CO2 and N2 permeabilities decreased to 25 % and 15 %, respectively. No plasticization effect of CO2 was observed under dry and wet conditions [69]. However, PIMs are known for their physical aging caused by the gradual collapse of the free volume elements, which could substantially decrease gas permeability in  time.  At  the  same  time,  their  sorption  properties  are  almost  unchanged [100].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Further, traditional commercial polymeric membranes [68] exhibit lower permeability than PIMs. A MMM based on PIM and the ' SNW-1 ' covalent  organic  framework  (COF)  was  synthesized  via  Schiff  base chemistry by Wu et al. [72]. SNW-1 expressively increased the diffusion process  of  CO2.  Mixed  gas  separation  experiments  and  pure  gas permeation  tests  showed  an  increase  of  27.4 % and  37.6  %  in  the selectivity of CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2, respectively, compared to the neat PIM membrane [69], along with an enhancement of 116  % in CO2 permeability  [72].  The  effect  on  polymer  microporosity  and  gas permeability by placing simple substituents such as methyl, t-butyl and fused benzo groups onto PIMs derived from spirobifluorene (PIM-SBFs) was reported by Bezzu et al. [73]. They showed that methyl or t-butyl substituents  both  cause  a  significant  increase  in  gas  permeabilities, enhancing the concentration of ultramicropores that contribute to selective  gas  transport.  The  incorporation  of  dimethylbenzotriptycene units in PIM-DM-BTrip was beneficial in increasing CO2/N2  selectivity [74]. Amine groups in amine-PIM-1, formed by converting the original nitrile groups in the polymer PIM-1, resulted in a higher CO2 uptake and higher CO2/N2  sorption selectivity, although the overall permeability was reduced [75].

Mixed  gas  separation  experiments  and  pure  gas permeation  tests  showed  an  increase  of  27.4 % and  37.6  %  in  the selectivity of CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2, respectively, compared to the neat PIM membrane [69], along with an enhancement of 116  % in CO2 permeability  [72].  The  effect  on  polymer  microporosity  and  gas permeability by placing simple substituents such as methyl, t-butyl and fused benzo groups onto PIMs derived from spirobifluorene (PIM-SBFs) was reported by Bezzu et al. [73]. They showed that methyl or t-butyl substituents  both  cause  a  significant  increase  in  gas  permeabilities, enhancing the concentration of ultramicropores that contribute to selective  gas  transport.  The  incorporation  of  dimethylbenzotriptycene units in PIM-DM-BTrip was beneficial in increasing CO2/N2  selectivity [74]. Amine groups in amine-PIM-1, formed by converting the original nitrile groups in the polymer PIM-1, resulted in a higher CO2 uptake and higher CO2/N2  sorption selectivity, although the overall permeability was reduced [75].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite advances in PIMs, only a few polymer materials are tested at a larger scale for flue gas purification, as shown in Fig. 9. Most of the commercially available membrane modules use flat membranes in the form  of  an  envelope  (Polyactive ™ ,  Helmholtz-Zentrum  Geesthacht, Germany)  or  spiral  wound  unit  (Polaris ™ ,  Membrane  Technology Research, USA) except for PRISM ® (Air  Products,  USA).  Surprisingly, several  test  results  with  facilitated-transport  membranes  have  better results than commercial units [101 -103].

texts.orig: 3.1.3. Mixed matrix membranes
texts.text: 3.1.3. Mixed matrix membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mixed  matrix  membranes  (MMMs)  are  presented  as  a  possible approach  to  achieve  improved  separation  performance  compared  to state-of-the-art  polymer  membranes  by  many  research  groups,  both from a computational viewpoint [104 -106] and from a practical point of view [1,107 -109]. The main reason to add filler materials to a polymer is to improve the transport properties directly, addressing the solutiondiffusion mechanism of polymeric membranes, or indirectly, influencing other transport-related properties of the polymers. In terms of membrane performance, the main targets of dispersed heterogeneous fillers are:

texts.orig: permeability, diffusivity, and solubility at various temperatures and feed pressures was studied. Increasing the nanoparticle load up to 5 wt % increased  the  CO2/N2 permselectivity  by  161 %,  while  the  CO2 permeability  decreased  by  only  3.3  % [79]. Fe(BTC),  an  iron-based MOF  with  1,3,5-benzene  tricarboxylate  (BTC)  linker,  was  evaluated for CO2/N2  separation. As an adsorbent, it proved selectivity towards CO2,  with higher selectivity reported at lower temperatures and pressures. It was also successfully incorporated into Matrimid ® 5218-based MMMs, and this type of membrane can be considered a potential option for application in post-combustion CO2  capture [80]. Fabrication of a new MMM using two microporous polymers: PIM-1 and a benzimidazole-linked  polymer  (BILP-101),  showed  high  CO2  permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity due to the inherent H-bonding capability of the constituent materials [81].

Increasing the nanoparticle load up to 5 wt % increased  the  CO2/N2 permselectivity  by  161 %,  while  the  CO2 permeability  decreased  by  only  3.3  % [79]. Fe(BTC),  an  iron-based MOF  with  1,3,5-benzene  tricarboxylate  (BTC)  linker,  was  evaluated for CO2/N2  separation. As an adsorbent, it proved selectivity towards CO2,  with higher selectivity reported at lower temperatures and pressures. It was also successfully incorporated into Matrimid ® 5218-based MMMs, and this type of membrane can be considered a potential option for application in post-combustion CO2  capture [80]. Fabrication of a new MMM using two microporous polymers: PIM-1 and a benzimidazole-linked  polymer  (BILP-101),  showed  high  CO2  permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity due to the inherent H-bonding capability of the constituent materials [81].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Glassy polymer Ultrason ® S 6010 (US-6010) with embedded nanocrystals  of  zeolitic  imidazolate  frameworks  (ZIF-300,  40  wt  %)  was tested under humid conditions. It showed better gas separation performance than the neat polymer, which was hardly affected by humidity [82]. MOFs were used as fillers to evaluate their performances in MMMs. The selection of MOF fillers with narrow pore sizes and low porosities leads to MMMs exhibiting high CO2/N2 selectivity [83]. Incorporating silica gel into a novel ionic cross-linked polyether membrane enhanced the CO2 permeability of the neat polymer membrane by 40 %, with no significant change in CO2/N2 selectivity. The silica filler also improved the thermal and mechanical properties, making MMMs more durable under practical gas separation conditions [84].
texts.text: Glassy polymer Ultrason ® S 6010 (US-6010) with embedded nanocrystals  of  zeolitic  imidazolate  frameworks  (ZIF-300,  40  wt  %)  was tested under humid conditions. It showed better gas separation performance than the neat polymer, which was hardly affected by humidity [82]. MOFs were used as fillers to evaluate their performances in MMMs. The selection of MOF fillers with narrow pore sizes and low porosities leads to MMMs exhibiting high CO2/N2 selectivity [83]. Incorporating silica gel into a novel ionic cross-linked polyether membrane enhanced the CO2 permeability of the neat polymer membrane by 40 %, with no significant change in CO2/N2 selectivity. The silica filler also improved the thermal and mechanical properties, making MMMs more durable under practical gas separation conditions [84].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Modelling and predicting membrane properties remain nontrivial, and one cannot describe these processes as routine exercises. Caleb and Lloyd used a mathematical model based on parallel-series arrangement of  the  main  structural  components  of  so-called  ZeoTIPS  membranes, namely polymer, and zeolite, in parallel with the void in series, and compared the results  with  the  ones  for  dense  MMMs  and  data  from relevant papers in the literature [118]. The modeling results predicted good oxygen/nitrogen selectivity and potential for overcoming the 2008 Robeson Plot upper bound for ZeoTIPS membranes compared to dense MMMs. The most remarkable improvement was prediction under the condition where the polymer phase is impermeable for the separated gaseous compound. The properties of MMMs were also discussed using the  Maxwell  equation  and  a  series/parallel  model  [119].  The  model results were compared with experimental results for PSF/PDMS composites. The main conclusions encompassed the roles of the polymer and the zeolite phases in the final predicted MMM performance: the polymer phase defines the minimum achievable performance, which the addition of the zeolite phase can improve. Grand canonical Monte Carlo simulations were combined with IR microscopy to predict adsorption and diffusion data on ZIF-8 [120]. The permeation selectivity was calculated

texts.text: 3.1.4. Facilitated transport

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent years, significant advances have been reached in facilitated transport membranes and fixed carrier membranes for CO2 separations. Combining these materials with tailor-made ILs for CO2 yields a promising  technology  platform  for  gas  treatment  processes,  such  as  postcombustion and direct air capture of CO2  (DAC) [123]. A recent overview  of  recently  facilitated  transport  membranes  based  on  fixed-site carriers is summarized in Table 6, and those using mobile carriers in Table 7. Both types of facilitated transport membranes are usually based on  polyvinylalcohol  (PVA)  or  polyvinyl  amine  (PVAm)  matrix.  The facilitated transport membranes mostly employ mobile carriers for CO2 salts of various naturally occurring amino acids.

texts.orig: Facilitated transport membranes could also be fabricated by incorporating IL functionalized GO (GO-IL) into standard elastomer e.g. poly (ether-block-amide) (Pebax ® 1657). In addition, existing IL-NH2 on the interspaces of GO nanosheets facilitated the transport of CO2 through a reversible reaction, which enhances over 90  % CO2/N2 selectivity and more than 50 % CO2  permeability [59]. Epoxide-amine PIL/IL ion-gel membranes  with  incorporated  facilitated  transport  groups  exhibit excellent CO2 transport under low pressure [88]. Membranes based on cellulose  acetate  (CA)  poly(ionic  liquids)  (poly(ILs))  modified  with pyrrolidinium cations show significant improvement (3-fold) in permeate flow and process stability for CO2/N2  compared to the reference  poly(IL)  [76].Pebax ® 1657/[emim][BF4]  gel  membranes  were fabricated for CO2  separation performance with mixed-gas feeds containing traces of water vapor and NOX. texts.label: text
texts.orig: were  obtained  in  the  pure  gas  permeation  measurements  [87].  An extremely promising so-called hybrid-integrated membrane, having a thin amine-functionalized facilitated transport layer incorporated into a highly permeable support membrane, was recently presented by Sandru et al. [146]. The hydrated amino groups provide exceptional CO2/N2 selectivity without significantly impacting the CO2  permeability of the underlying support. As additives for CO2  capture, Pebax ® membranes were doped with [ α / α -N α -Bn-hydrazino] pseudo-peptide-polymer bioconjugates. The addition of tetramer-based bioconjugate at 3 eq. wt.% result in a CO2 permeability of 194 Barrer ( + 46 % compared to that of Pebax ® ) and constant selectivity ( α (CO2/N2) = 44 and α (CO2/CH4) = 13) [77]. A combination of polymers and different IL ' s with high CO2 solubility and selectivity has been extensively studied in recent years to improve the performance of CO2  separation  membranes [122]. Ilyas et al. [85] combined 3-amino propyltrimethoxysilane with acetic acid and produced a protic IL membrane, showing high CO2 absorption capacity  and  affinity  for  CO2 separation.  The  synergistic  effect  of  the methoxy groups from the cation and the subtle acid/base interaction of CO2 with the anion ensures an inherent facilitated transport of CO2. This interaction could lead to the effective separation of CO2  even at relatively high temperatures [85]. Membranes for CO2 separation from H2, N2, and CH4 were fabricated using poly(tetramethylene oxide) (PTMO) and Pebax ® 1657. Permeation results showed an increment in the tested gases and an enhancement in CO2/H2 selectivity in the presence of the additive. Diffusion coefficients were enhanced depending on the penetrants ' molecular size. Since CH4 and N2 diffusion increased more than that of CO2, this resulted in lower CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 permselectivity values [78].
texts.text: were  obtained  in  the  pure  gas  permeation  measurements  [87]. [146]. The hydrated amino groups provide exceptional CO2/N2 selectivity without significantly impacting the CO2  permeability of the underlying support. As additives for CO2  capture, Pebax ® membranes were doped with [ α / α -N α -Bn-hydrazino] pseudo-peptide-polymer bioconjugates. The addition of tetramer-based bioconjugate at 3 eq. wt.% result in a CO2 permeability of 194 Barrer ( + 46 % compared to that of Pebax ® ) and constant selectivity ( α (CO2/N2) = 44 and α (CO2/CH4) = 13) [77]. A combination of polymers and different IL ' s with high CO2 solubility and selectivity has been extensively studied in recent years to improve the performance of CO2  separation  membranes [122]. Ilyas et al. [85] combined 3-amino propyltrimethoxysilane with acetic acid and produced a protic IL membrane, showing high CO2 absorption capacity  and  affinity  for  CO2 separation.  The  synergistic  effect  of  the methoxy groups from the cation and the subtle acid/base interaction of CO2 with the anion ensures an inherent facilitated transport of CO2. This interaction could lead to the effective separation of CO2  even at relatively high temperatures [85]. Membranes for CO2 separation from H2, N2, and CH4 were fabricated using poly(tetramethylene oxide) (PTMO) and Pebax ® 1657. Permeation results showed an increment in the tested gases and an enhancement in CO2/H2 selectivity in the presence of the additive. Diffusion coefficients were enhanced depending on the penetrants ' molecular size. Since CH4 and N2 diffusion increased more than that of CO2, this resulted in lower CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 permselectivity values [78].

texts.orig: An overview of membrane contactors for CO2 separation is shown in Table 8. At low inlet CO2  partial pressures, chemical absorbents can significantly increase reaction rates with CO2. Different solutions can be used as absorbents. The most promising ones are the aqueous solutions of  alkanolamines  (e.g.,  monoethanolamine  (MEA).  The  efficiency  of such solutions is strictly related to the concentration of reactive species present.  Indeed,  the  absorption  capacity  of  chemical  absorbents  decreases dramatically after saturation by CO2 molecules.
texts.text: An overview of membrane contactors for CO2 separation is shown in Table 8. At low inlet CO2  partial pressures, chemical absorbents can significantly increase reaction rates with CO2. Different solutions can be used as absorbents. The most promising ones are the aqueous solutions of  alkanolamines  (e.g.,  monoethanolamine  (MEA).  The  efficiency  of such solutions is strictly related to the concentration of reactive species present.  Indeed,  the  absorption  capacity  of  chemical  absorbents  decreases dramatically after saturation by CO2 molecules.

texts.orig: With membranes in membrane contactors, swelling of the membrane increases the mass transfer resistance and annihilates process intensification performances. PP membrane shows a high sensitivity to wetting effects when exposed to MEA solutions over a long time scale [152]. PTFE fibres resist wetting effects relatively well [148] but are significantly more expensive. The most cost-effective is the PVDF membrane [150] which has comparable performance to the PTFE membrane [149]. However, one must be careful because PVDF and its copolymers may be completely wetted and absorb certain ILs spontaneously [155].

The  IL,  upon  recirculation,  absorbs  CO2 until  reaching  a pseudo-steady state [153]. A significant advantage of using ILs as the liquid sorbent is that their non-volatile nature allows a vacuum-driven desorption step [156].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.1. Inorganic membrane contactors
texts.text: 4.1.1. Inorganic membrane contactors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ceramic membrane contactors are considered adequate for SO2 absorption and subsequent removal. The size of the membrane element and  module  significantly  impacts  the  so-called  critical  gas  velocity, which is defined as the gas velocity for which the SO2 removal efficiency is 95 % or higher [170]. A further increase in the gas velocity above this critical  value  decreases  the  removal  rate.  This  parameter  is  strongly related to structural parameters, including membrane  thickness, porosity, tubular diameter, and packing density. This parameter helps to optimize the gas velocity in different membrane contactors. These results imply that adding baffles improves the membrane ' s desulfurization performance  [157].  A  novel  hydrophilic  ceramic  (Al2O3)  membrane contactor  with  an  aqueous  sodium  hydroxide  (NaOH)  solution  was implemented  for  SO2 absorption.  Absorbent  temperature  plays  an essential role in the mass transfer behaviour. An increase in the absorbent temperature from 10 ◦ C to 35 ◦ C resulted in the SO2 removal efficiency and the SO2 mass transfer flux increasing from 48.6 % to 88.2 % (i.e., from 0.094 mol.m 2 h 1 to 0.156 mol.m 2 h 1 , respectively) [159].

texts.orig: 4.1.2. Polymer membrane contactors
texts.text: 4.1.2. Polymer membrane contactors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hydrophobic membrane materials like PP, PTFE, and PVDF may be promising candidates for SO2  absorption. Porous polymer membranes made from PVDF and PTFE were used as contactors to remove SO2, with 82  %  and  88  %  efficiencies,  respectively  [161].  In  this  study,  with increasing the feed flow rate and the SO2  concentration, the removal efficiency  decreases  due  to  corrosion  of  the  membrane  contactors ' potting material.
texts.text: Hydrophobic membrane materials like PP, PTFE, and PVDF may be promising candidates for SO2  absorption. Porous polymer membranes made from PVDF and PTFE were used as contactors to remove SO2, with 82  %  and  88  %  efficiencies,  respectively  [161].  In  this  study,  with increasing the feed flow rate and the SO2  concentration, the removal efficiency  decreases  due  to  corrosion  of  the  membrane  contactors ' potting material.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A promising alternative to conventional wet scrubbing technologies for flue gas purification is a PP membrane contactor with aqueous MEA as  the  absorbent  solution.  Such  membrane  contactors  were  able  to capture  most  of  the  SO2 from  a  mixed-gas  stream  [162].  In  other research,  the  authors  developed  an  efficient  flue  gas  desulfurization process, employing a hydrophobic PP-HFMC membrane, using deionized water as a scrubbing liquid. The SO2 removal efficiency decreased gradually as the temperature increased from 283 to 333 K based on model predictions [163]. Park et al. tested PVDF hollow fibre membranes  prepared  from  a  PVDF  solution  in  N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP). Their gas -liquid contactor absorbed SO2  from flue gas with an SO2 removal efficiency reaching 85 % [164].

Such  membrane  contactors  were  able  to capture  most  of  the  SO2 from  a  mixed-gas  stream  [162]. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.3. MMM contactors
texts.text: 4.1.3. MMM contactors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, MMM-based contractors were successfully tested for flue gas desulphurization with the addition of MOFs. Zhang et al. investigated the impact of the module parameters on SO2  removal inside a membrane  where  dimethylaniline  was  used  as  the  absorbent  liquid [166].  The  contactor  performance  was  improved  by  increasing  the membrane  porosity  and  decreasing  the  membrane  thickness.  The improved module provides the best SO2  absorption and can simultaneously absorb two acidic gases (CO2 and SO2). In another study, using a PVDF-based MMM contactor that contained a core -shell  hierarchical Cu@4A composite filler (Cu@4A), the SO2 removal efficiency and SO2 absorption flux were increased compared with a PVDF/SiO2 membrane [168]. By depositing fluorinated silica particles on the top surface of a PES membrane, a single-sided superhydrophobic fluorinated silica/poly (ether sulfone) (fSiO2/PES) membrane was prepared for SO2 absorption.
texts.text: Recently, MMM-based contractors were successfully tested for flue gas desulphurization with the addition of MOFs. Zhang et al. investigated the impact of the module parameters on SO2  removal inside a membrane  where  dimethylaniline  was  used  as  the  absorbent  liquid [166].  The  contactor  performance  was  improved  by  increasing  the membrane  porosity  and  decreasing  the  membrane  thickness.  The improved module provides the best SO2  absorption and can simultaneously absorb two acidic gases (CO2 and SO2). In another study, using a PVDF-based MMM contactor that contained a core -shell  hierarchical Cu@4A composite filler (Cu@4A), the SO2 removal efficiency and SO2 absorption flux were increased compared with a PVDF/SiO2 membrane [168]. By depositing fluorinated silica particles on the top surface of a PES membrane, a single-sided superhydrophobic fluorinated silica/poly (ether sulfone) (fSiO2/PES) membrane was prepared for SO2 absorption.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.4. Facilitated-transport membrane contactors
texts.text: 4.1.4. Facilitated-transport membrane contactors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Facilitated transport membranes, using carriers that react selectively and reversibly with the permeant species, have attracted attention since very high selectivity is obtained compared with conventional polymeric membranes and membrane contactors, especially at low partial pressures  of  permeant  species.  Concerning  the  separation  of  SO2 using facilitated transport membranes, it was found that the permeability of SO2 through membranes containing water, aqueous salt solutions (e.g., NaCl), and aqueous NaOH solutions as a carrier is much higher [174]. Also, facilitated transport membranes with an ionic carrier (Cu 2 + , Cr 3 + ) are suitable for removing SO2 since the carrier increases the solubility of the chemical species in the membrane or promotes the transfer speed and faster diffusion through the membranes (Table 9).
texts.text: Facilitated transport membranes, using carriers that react selectively and reversibly with the permeant species, have attracted attention since very high selectivity is obtained compared with conventional polymeric membranes and membrane contactors, especially at low partial pressures  of  permeant  species.  Concerning  the  separation  of  SO2 using facilitated transport membranes, it was found that the permeability of SO2 through membranes containing water, aqueous salt solutions (e.g., NaCl), and aqueous NaOH solutions as a carrier is much higher [174]. Also, facilitated transport membranes with an ionic carrier (Cu 2 + , Cr 3 + ) are suitable for removing SO2 since the carrier increases the solubility of the chemical species in the membrane or promotes the transfer speed and faster diffusion through the membranes (Table 9).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A PVDF-based MMM with composite filler (Cu@4A) was prepared by Zhang et al. A core -shell  hierarchical Cu@4A composite filler at the polymer-filler interface tends to enhance the gas permeation due to the facilitated transport pathway. Membranes show increased chemical SO2 absorption  flux  and  demonstrate  the  highest  adsorption  capacity  of 27.08 mg SO2/g [168]. PVDF membranes with Cr 3 + carriers offer high efficiency in SO2 removal, providing more SO2 channels to enhance SO2 absorption flux. The incorporation of MIL-101(Cr) noticeably enhances the  membrane ' s  desulfurization  performance,  which  results  from  the abundant  SO2  affinity  sites  and  multiple  SO2-philic  channels  formed between the MIL-101(Cr) and PVDF interface [169].

The incorporation of MIL-101(Cr) noticeably enhances the  membrane ' s  desulfurization  performance,  which  results  from  the abundant  SO2  affinity  sites  and  multiple  SO2-philic  channels  formed between the MIL-101(Cr) and PVDF interface [169].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.5. SILMs as contactors
texts.text: 4.1.5. SILMs as contactors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SILMs  consist  of  a  porous  membrane  in  which  the  pores  are impregnated with an IL with an affinity for the gas of interest. Removal of  SO2 from  gas  mixtures  by  selective  absorption  into  a  liquid  is  a standard method to separate and concentrate SO2 for industrial uses. Use of  an  IL  as  the  absorption  solvent  avoids  solvent  losses  due  to  fluid

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.6. Hollow fibre membrane contactors
texts.text: 4.1.6. Hollow fibre membrane contactors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A novel hydrophilic ceramic (Al2O3) membrane contactor with an aqueous  NaOH  solution  was  implemented  for  SO2 absorption.  This membrane  exhibited  exceptional  long-term  stability  under  480  h continuous  operation,  and  mass  transfer  flux  increased  from  48.6  to 88.2 % [159]. Luis et al. [160] used an Al2O3  hollow fibre membrane contactor to remove SO2 from gas streams in DMA and discussed technical, environmental, and economic issues for industrial application.
