texts.orig: 4.2. Gas/gas separation
texts.text: 4.2. Gas/gas separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of mixed-gas SO2/CO2 separation (see examples of used membranes for the separation Table 11), the SO2 permeance is likely to be  significantly  lower  than  that  of  the  pure  gas  due  to  the  limited availability of sorption sites, which are reduced in the presence of the other gas [5].
texts.text: In the case of mixed-gas SO2/CO2 separation (see examples of used membranes for the separation Table 11), the SO2 permeance is likely to be  significantly  lower  than  that  of  the  pure  gas  due  to  the  limited availability of sorption sites, which are reduced in the presence of the other gas [5].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.1. Polymer membranes
texts.text: 4.2.1. Polymer membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several polymer membranes are selective for SO2  relative to other gases because of their strong condensability for SO2. An acid gas separation  process  usually  demonstrates  a  preference  for  SO2  separation before CO2 because of its higher condensability. The structure of polymer chains  has  an  impact  on  SO2  selectivity.  For  example,  the  high permeability and permselectivity of polarizable gases through Pebax ® copolymer  are  attributed  to  the  strong  affinity  to  the  PE  block  in Pebax ® .  The  plasticization  of  the  membrane  evoked  by  condensable components in PEI/ Pebax ® 657 and PEI/PDMS/ Pebax ® 1657/PDMS composite membranes [182,183] tends to increase the permeance of SO2. Two TR membranes formed from the precursor co-polyimide 4,4 ′ -(hexafluoro  isopropylidene)diphthalic  anhydride  (6FDA),  3,3 ′ -dihydroxyl-4,4 ′ -diamino diphenyl (HAB) and 2,4,6-trimethyl-m-phenylenediamine  (DAM)  (6FDA-HAB-DAM)  with  3,5-diamino  benzoic  acid (DABA) crosslinker display lower SO2/CO2 selectivity than other polymers  of  similar  permeability.  The  SO2 permeability  decreases  with increasing SO2 partial pressure from 0.2 to 0.9 kPa for both membranes because  of  SO2  saturation  in  the  Langmuir  microvoids  [176].  In  the ultra-permeable  PIM  (PIM-TMN-Trip),  CO2 and  SO2 are  removed simultaneously from flue gas, with a permeability of ca. 3 × 10 4 barrer for both gases [71].
texts.text: Several polymer membranes are selective for SO2  relative to other gases because of their strong condensability for SO2. An acid gas separation  process  usually  demonstrates  a  preference  for  SO2  separation before CO2 because of its higher condensability. For  example,  the  high permeability and permselectivity of polarizable gases through Pebax ® copolymer  are  attributed  to  the  strong  affinity  to  the  PE  block  in Pebax ® .  The  plasticization  of  the  membrane  evoked  by  condensable components in PEI/ Pebax ® 657 and PEI/PDMS/ Pebax ® 1657/PDMS composite membranes [182,183] tends to increase the permeance of SO2. Two TR membranes formed from the precursor co-polyimide 4,4 ′ -(hexafluoro  isopropylidene)diphthalic  anhydride  (6FDA),  3,3 ′ -dihydroxyl-4,4 ′ -diamino diphenyl (HAB) and 2,4,6-trimethyl-m-phenylenediamine  (DAM)  (6FDA-HAB-DAM)  with  3,5-diamino  benzoic  acid (DABA) crosslinker display lower SO2/CO2 selectivity than other polymers  of  similar  permeability.  The  SO2 permeability  decreases  with increasing SO2 partial pressure from 0.2 to 0.9 kPa for both membranes because  of  SO2  saturation  in  the  Langmuir  microvoids  [176].  In  the ultra-permeable  PIM  (PIM-TMN-Trip),  CO2 and  SO2 are  removed simultaneously from flue gas, with a permeability of ca. 3 × 10 4 barrer for both gases [71].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cellulose triacetate (CTA) membranes occupy much of the gas separation  market  in  natural  gas  processing.  The  main  advantages  are selectivity for SO2  relative to other significant gases due to the strong condensability of SO2 [177]. The impacts of liquid water condensate of variable pH, SO2,  and NOx  on the gas separation performance of CTA membrane  are  of  critical  interest  to  ensure  maximum  membrane lifetime.

The  main  advantages  are selectivity for SO2  relative to other significant gases due to the strong condensability of SO2 [177]. The impacts of liquid water condensate of variable pH, SO2,  and NOx  on the gas separation performance of CTA membrane  are  of  critical  interest  to  ensure  maximum  membrane lifetime.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeability of SO2  within a composite has been reported by Kuehne and Friedlander, who focused on the impact of SO2 pressure and membrane  fabrication  technique  [178].  CTA/polyacrylate  composite membranes have properties that make them attractive for separating SO2 from a mixture of gases. Polyacrylate was 10 times more permeable than CTA, so in the composite membrane, the SO2 permeability was not dependent on pressure.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.2. SILMs
texts.text: 4.2.2. SILMs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Jiang et al. [111] have measured the permeability of SO2 in imidazolium  ILs  supported  on  the  PES  microfiltration  membranes.  SO2 permeability across increases with the increase of gas partial pressure. SO2  permeability in [bmim][Tf2N] increases by 1206 barrer when the transmembrane pressure rises from 10 to 50 kPa. This is consistent with the  SO2 solubility  increasing  with  the  driving  force.  Thus,  the  SO2 permeability in [bmim][Tf2N] is 7 -10 times as large as that of CO2. In [emim][BF4]  membranes,  SO2  permeates  18  times  faster  than  CO2, which indicates that the supported [emim][BF4] membrane has a higher SO2 selectivity than other SILMs [179].

SO2 permeability across increases with the increase of gas partial pressure. SO2  permeability in [bmim][Tf2N] increases by 1206 barrer when the transmembrane pressure rises from 10 to 50 kPa. This is consistent with the  SO2 solubility  increasing  with  the  driving  force.  Thus,  the  SO2 permeability in [bmim][Tf2N] is 7 -10 times as large as that of CO2. In [emim][BF4]  membranes,  SO2  permeates  18  times  faster  than  CO2, which indicates that the supported [emim][BF4] membrane has a higher SO2 selectivity than other SILMs [179].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.3. Facilitated -transport membranes
texts.text: 4.2.3. Facilitated -transport membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There  are  few  reports  on  the  facilitated  separation  of  SO2.  Triethylbutylammonium dimalonate ([N2224][dimalonate]) could facilitate the permeation of SO2, characterized by lower permeability of SO2  in comparison with [emim][CF3SO3] and [emim][CF3SO3] + GPA mixture. The reason may be that although [N2224][dimalonate] could reversibly react with SO2 and act as a carrier for the transport of SO2, its viscosity (170 mPa s) is higher than that of [emim][CF3SO3] and [emim] [CF3SO3] + GPA mixture ( < 100 mPa s). However, the permselectivity of SO2/CO2  in [N2224][dimalonate] is much higher than that in [emim] [CF3SO3]  and  [emim][CF3SO3] + GPA  mixture.  Therefore,  [N2224] [dimalonate] could be an efficient medium for selectively separating SO2 from other gases [181].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Removal of NOX
texts.text: 5. Removal of NOX

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.1. Gas/gas separation
texts.text: 5.1. Gas/gas separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Selective  catalytic  reduction  (SCR)  and  selective  non-catalytic reduction (SNCR) are the main methods used today that reduce NOx content  to  a  minimum.  However,  membrane  permeation  of  NOx  is lacking attention from the research community. Scholes reviewed the effects of minor components such as NOx [184], and little information on their interactions with polymeric membranes was presented in the article. The data provided show that the average concentration range of NOx in the combustion gas mixture is from 100 to 500 ppm. Nonetheless, NOx  form corrosive substances and thus can cause corrosion of steel components. NOx  compounds also influence the surface chemistry of membranes  and  can  affect  membrane  permeability  for  certain  other constituents of the gaseous mixture. Table 12 summarizes the recent and historical  publications  on  NOx  separations.  The  membranes  can  be divided into three groups: zeolite membranes, composite zeolite polymer membranes, and polymer membranes. A separate small group of membranes  represents  high-temperature  ceramic  membranes  with supported molten electrolytes.
texts.text: Selective  catalytic  reduction  (SCR)  and  selective  non-catalytic reduction (SNCR) are the main methods used today that reduce NOx content  to  a  minimum.  However,  membrane  permeation  of  NOx  is lacking attention from the research community. Scholes reviewed the effects of minor components such as NOx [184], and little information on their interactions with polymeric membranes was presented in the article. The data provided show that the average concentration range of NOx in the combustion gas mixture is from 100 to 500 ppm. Nonetheless, NOx  form corrosive substances and thus can cause corrosion of steel components. NOx  compounds also influence the surface chemistry of membranes  and  can  affect  membrane  permeability  for  certain  other constituents of the gaseous mixture. Table 12 summarizes the recent and historical  publications  on  NOx  separations.  The  membranes  can  be divided into three groups: zeolite membranes, composite zeolite polymer membranes, and polymer membranes. A separate small group of membranes  represents  high-temperature  ceramic  membranes  with supported molten electrolytes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zeolite  membranes  represent  one  of  the  most  modern  and  novel groups of membranes for nitrogen oxides separations. Nevertheless, only a few papers focus on separating this minor component of exhaust fumes and combustion gases. The SSZ-13 membrane was first reported as a zeolite-based membrane for N2/NO2  separation [186]. The membrane properties were systematically adjusted by a combination of two templates N, N, N -trimethtyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH) and tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) in various ratios to achieve continuous polycrystalline membrane of desired separation ability and lower technologically possible thickness (2 µ m). The authors used the same type of zeolite membrane [187], where the size exclusion sieve effect of the zeolite membrane enabled the separation of the dimer of NO2  from the mixture with SO2. The nitric oxide undergoes a dimerization reaction under elevated pressure and temperature conditions. The paper reports good results for the permeance and separation factor of SO2/NO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: [191] to separate nitrogen oxides in a mixture with nitrogen and Ar. The changes in the membrane surface chemistry and transport mechanisms for each gas component were investigated. It was found that the transport  of  NO2 was  concentration-dependent  and  thermally  activated. Gradual alteration of the membrane in the presence of NOx caused the reduction of the permeation of the weakly adsorbed and inert components. DD3R zeolite membrane [196] was used to separate numerous gas pairs, including CO2, N2O, CH4, N2, and O2, as well as the noble gases Ar and Kr. N2O was separated from the air with a selectivity ranging from 20 to 400. The authors also elaborated on transport mechanisms influencing  membrane  separation,  such  as  size  exclusion,  adsorption, competitive adsorption effects, and gas/zeolite cage interactions [196].
texts.text: [191] to separate nitrogen oxides in a mixture with nitrogen and Ar. The changes in the membrane surface chemistry and transport mechanisms for each gas component were investigated. It was found that the transport  of  NO2 was  concentration-dependent  and  thermally  activated. Gradual alteration of the membrane in the presence of NOx caused the reduction of the permeation of the weakly adsorbed and inert components. DD3R zeolite membrane [196] was used to separate numerous gas pairs, including CO2, N2O, CH4, N2, and O2, as well as the noble gases Ar and Kr. N2O was separated from the air with a selectivity ranging from 20 to 400. The authors also elaborated on transport mechanisms influencing  membrane  separation,  such  as  size  exclusion,  adsorption, competitive adsorption effects, and gas/zeolite cage interactions [196].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A second novel group of membranes for NOx separation is supported by  molten  electrolyte  ceramic  membranes  operated  at  elevated  temperatures.  A  hollow-fibre  multichannel  system  with  finger-like  pores filled with molten nitrate salt was investigated for the permeation of NOx. The authors investigated the influence of the nitric oxide content, total  feed  flow,  and  temperature  on  the  total  permeance  of  the  NOx mixture. It was found that the permeance increases with temperature and total feed flow but decreases with the increasing content of nitric oxide [188]. Molten potassium nitrate-filled ceramic membrane systems consisting  of  KNO3/Al2O3 and  KNO3/La0.6Sr0.4Co0.2  Fe0.8O3 δ (LSCF) were reported as being successful and promising candidates for selective separation of nitrogen oxides. A KNO3/La0.6Sr0.4Co0.2  Fe0.8O3 δ (LSCF) membrane demonstrated a  decreasing  NOx/O2  separation  factor  and NOx permeance, respectively, with an increasing molar fraction of NO2. KNO3/Al2O3 displays no co-permeation of NOx and O2 compared to the other membrane system [190].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  third  group  is  represented  by  polymer  and  composite  membranes, historically the oldest but with the greatest abundance of used materials.  The  materials  include  fluorinated  ethylene-propylene,  PSf, Matrimid ® 5218, and 6FDA-TMPDA, poly(dimethyl siloxanecodiphenyl  siloxane)  copolymer,  silicone  rubber,  cellulose  acetate,  and PTFE.  The  higher  gas  permeability  of  NO  coincides  with  increasing critical gas temperature in the order N2 < NO, resulting from the higher affinity of NO for the polymeric matrices and, thus, a higher solubility [185].

texts.orig: While the novel types of membranes rely on size exclusion, structure/gas interaction, and selective chemisorption (zeolite-based membranes) or facilitated transport (molten electrolyte-supported membranes), the older polymer and composited membranes rely on the solution-diffusion mechanism. The comparison of the separation performance based on the reviewed publications is somewhat problematic due to  the  heterogeneity  in  the  reported  information  on  permeance, permeability, and selectivity. Some publications report the NOx  mixture ' s  total  flux  and  permeability  rather  than  particular  single  oxide molecules. There is also significant variability in the type of sweep gas

texts.orig: 5.2. Gas separation using liquids
texts.text: 5.2. Gas separation using liquids

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Leading research groups [197,198] evaluated a hollow-fibre membrane bioreactor (HFMB) for removing NO from combustion gases. Due to the hollow-fibre membrane module ' s unique characteristics, the gas and liquid phases can flow in the membrane module ' s lumen and shell sides. It was reported that an HFMB could remove NO using nitrifying bacteria that convert NO to nitrate (NO3 -) and other products. Nitrate could lead to eutrophication and might require further treatment.
texts.text: Leading research groups [197,198] evaluated a hollow-fibre membrane bioreactor (HFMB) for removing NO from combustion gases. Due to the hollow-fibre membrane module ' s unique characteristics, the gas and liquid phases can flow in the membrane module ' s lumen and shell sides. It was reported that an HFMB could remove NO using nitrifying bacteria that convert NO to nitrate (NO3 -) and other products. Nitrate could lead to eutrophication and might require further treatment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In contrast, NO was converted to harmless N2 through partial denitrification [199]. It was not necessary to treat the nitrogen further. Thus, the removal of NO via denitrification was a better alternative. Hollow fibre membranes supported the microbial populations and provided a large  surface  area  for  NO  and  O2  mass  transfer.  The  HFMB  process consists  of  bundles  of  microporous  hydrophobic  hollow-fibre  membranes, a biofilm containing nitrifying organisms, and a nutrient supply. The synthetic combustion gas (15 % CO2, 5  % O2, 74 % N2, 100 ppm NO)  containing  NO  passes  through  the  microporous  hydrophobic hollow-fibre  membrane ' s  lumen.  The  NO  gas  diffuses  through  the membrane pores and partitions  into  a  nitrifying  biofilm,  where  it  is oxidized to NO3 .  Over 90 % of the nitrogen is converted to NO3 by nitrification. The NO removal rate is 12 -14 g m 3  day 1 , corresponding to 34  % and 40  % efficiencies. It increases to a maximum of 27 g m 3 day 1 with 74 % removal efficiency at a recirculation rate of 600 cm 3 min 1 at 20 ◦ C. The NO elimination capacity ranges between 24 and 26 g m 3 day 1 at 20 ◦ C. The performance remained constant when the temperature was gradually increased from 20 to 30, 40, 50, and 55 ◦ C. The results showed that gas composition and temperature do not influence the reactor ' s performance [199] (Table 13).
texts.text: In contrast, NO was converted to harmless N2 through partial denitrification [199]. It was not necessary to treat the nitrogen further. Thus, the removal of NO via denitrification was a better alternative. The  NO  gas  diffuses  through  the membrane pores and partitions  into  a  nitrifying  biofilm,  where  it  is oxidized to NO3 .  Over 90 % of the nitrogen is converted to NO3 by nitrification. The NO removal rate is 12 -14 g m 3  day 1 , corresponding to 34  % and 40  % efficiencies. It increases to a maximum of 27 g m 3 day 1 with 74 % removal efficiency at a recirculation rate of 600 cm 3 min 1 at 20 ◦ C. The NO elimination capacity ranges between 24 and 26 g m 3 day 1 at 20 ◦ C. The performance remained constant when the temperature was gradually increased from 20 to 30, 40, 50, and 55 ◦ C. The results showed that gas composition and temperature do not influence the reactor ' s performance [199] (Table 13).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to Kyung-Nan Min et al. [200], NO diffuses through the membrane pores and partitions into a nitrifying biofilm, oxidizing to NO3 -and other products. The mixture of NO/air was introduced to the HFMB at ambient temperature. The maximum removal efficiency of 74 % was attained at 20 ◦ C and 55 ◦ C. NO removal efficiency was maintained at 70 % regardless of the gas composition or temperature.

The mixture of NO/air was introduced to the HFMB at ambient temperature. The maximum removal efficiency of 74 % was attained at 20 ◦ C and 55 ◦ C. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Removal of NO by a denitrification pathway in an HFMB showed that N2O and N2  production varied during NO reduction by the inoculum from the HFMB. In the later stage, the accumulated amount of N2O gradually decreased. The amount of N2 gradually increased during the whole experiment process. The volume ratio of N2  to N2O was about 2.3:1 at 36 h and remained constant afterward. These results suggest that the bioreactor ' s inoculum directly reduced NO to N2O and N2 in the vials.  This  could  partially  confirm  that  most  microorganisms  in  the HMBR were denitrifying bacteria [198].

texts.orig: Under various conditions, NOx  removal performance by the denitrification process in an HFMB using a nonporous poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) hollow-fibre membrane. The removal efficiency of such a membrane dramatically declined from 60.4 % to 37.6 % [204]. The same principle also works if dense PDMS membranes are used instead of porous hydrophobic membranes. Razaviarani et al. [203] showed that NO could be an effective electron acceptor for organic carbon biodegradation under denitrifying conditions. As the NO load increased, the organic carbon degradation also increased.

However,  it  was  shown  that  NO  could  easily  be removed in biological reactors, as described here [203]. A significant challenge, which was not addressed, is that NOx gases from combustion processes are emitted at high temperatures (i.e., ~200 ◦ C). texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Conclusions and outlook
texts.text: 6. Conclusions and outlook

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the numerous studies reported to date, it can be concluded that different membranes in their various forms, with a wide range of chemical,  physical,  and  transport  properties  and  different  operation principles, offer an excellent platform for effective gas separation. While the  performance  of  dense  films  has  been  extensively  explored  (predominantly with single gases and CO2 providing permeability and ideal selectivity data), there are still opportunities for more studies of mixed gas separation performance under more-realistic operating conditions at different temperatures, pressures, and feed compositions. The present review shows that membrane permeability studies with SO2 and NOx are scarce and usually not fully explored in the industry concerning temperature or gas mixture composition. In particular, the effects of humidity and trace amounts of other impurities, such as H2S, hydrocarbon vapours, and other source-specific chemicals (e.g., NH3, N2O, HCl) need further study. New studies should also focus on the stability of achievable permeance because many membrane materials with high permeability often undergo physical aging, resulting in a gradual and usually unwanted  decrease  in  permeability.  We  must  also  realize  that  the practical purification of flue gases involves huge gas volumes, and thus the  compression  work  during  their  compression  would  be  very economically demanding. Therefore, only membranes with extremely high  permeability  of  unwanted  gases  (i.e.,  CO2,  SO2,  NOx,  H2S,  and water vapor) have the potential for industrial scale-up. Preparing thinfilm composite membranes with ultra-high permeability based on materials such as PIMs, MMMs, and SILMs or using facilitated transport membranes  with  fixed-site  carriers  may  have  a  bright  future.  They should  be  further  explored  as  flat-sheet  membrane  films  (as  spiral wound modules or frame-to-frame modules) or hollow fibre modules. Also, it is crucial to focus on new technology arrangements and not only on new material development.
texts.text: Based on the numerous studies reported to date, it can be concluded that different membranes in their various forms, with a wide range of chemical,  physical,  and  transport  properties  and  different  operation principles, offer an excellent platform for effective gas separation. While the  performance  of  dense  films  has  been  extensively  explored  (predominantly with single gases and CO2 providing permeability and ideal selectivity data), there are still opportunities for more studies of mixed gas separation performance under more-realistic operating conditions at different temperatures, pressures, and feed compositions. The present review shows that membrane permeability studies with SO2 and NOx are scarce and usually not fully explored in the industry concerning temperature or gas mixture composition. In particular, the effects of humidity and trace amounts of other impurities, such as H2S, hydrocarbon vapours, and other source-specific chemicals (e.g., NH3, N2O, HCl) need further study. New studies should also focus on the stability of achievable permeance because many membrane materials with high permeability often undergo physical aging, resulting in a gradual and usually unwanted  decrease  in  permeability.  We  must  also  realize  that  the practical purification of flue gases involves huge gas volumes, and thus the  compression  work  during  their  compression  would  be  very economically demanding. Therefore, only membranes with extremely high  permeability  of  unwanted  gases  (i.e.,  CO2,  SO2,  NOx,  H2S,  and water vapor) have the potential for industrial scale-up. Preparing thinfilm composite membranes with ultra-high permeability based on materials such as PIMs, MMMs, and SILMs or using facilitated transport membranes  with  fixed-site  carriers  may  have  a  bright  future.  They should  be  further  explored  as  flat-sheet  membrane  films  (as  spiral wound modules or frame-to-frame modules) or hollow fibre modules. Also, it is crucial to focus on new technology arrangements and not only on new material development.

• Low cost; • Easy technology of fabrication; • Operating at low temperatures; • Less stringent demands for the materials required in the module construction. • Processability
• Manufacturing cost of the membranes ' preparation and modules; • Limited membrane lifetime; • Less resistant to high temperatures and aggressive chemicals.
• High permeability; • Improved selectivity; • Removal of specific contaminants; • Highly functional transport properties.

• Particle sedimentation and agglomeration; • Interfacial voids; • Filler - polymer incompatibility; • Complex interactions between all parameters can seriously affect the performance of the membrane.
• Improvement of selectivity without compromising on permeability.
• Lower stability; • Carrier leakage; • Saturation of carrier; • Require a highly controlled environment of operation
